text: Live-high train-low (LHTL), live-high train-high (LHTH), and intermittent hypoxic training (IHT) all significantly improve VO2max compared to normoxic (sea-level) training in athletes.
LHTL combined with low-altitude training is the most effective hypoxic intervention for improving VO2max, outperforming LHTL combined with sea-level training.
Natural altitude LHTL with low-altitude training is slightly more effective than simulated altitude LHTL with low-altitude training for improving VO2max.
The optimal hypoxic dose for live-high train-high (LHTH) follows an inverted U-shaped curve, with a reasonable range of 470-1,130 kilometer-hours.
The optimal hypoxic dose for live-high train-low (LHTL) follows an inverted U-shaped curve, with a reasonable range of 500-1,415 kilometer-hours.
Exceeding the optimal hypoxic dose range for LHTH and LHTL leads to diminishing or negative returns on VO2max improvement, likely due to the negative effects of chronic hypoxia.
Intermittent hypoxic exposure (IHE) alone does not significantly improve VO2max compared to normoxic training.
